subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Mighty Cable Company Cost 6√x $/meter, Revenue $120/meter — Profit, Integral Meaning, Expression & Max Profit
stimulus: Mighty Cable Company manufactures cable that sells for $120 per meter. For a cable of fixed length, the cost of producing a portion of the cable varies with its distance from the beginning of the cable. Mighty reports that the cost to produce a portion of a cable that is $x$ meters from the beginning of the cable is $6\sqrt{x}$ dollars per meter. (Note: Profit is defined to be the difference between the amount of money received by the company for selling the cable and the company's cost of producing the cable.)

parts.prompt: Find Mighty's profit on the sale of a 25-meter cable.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Profit $= 120 \cdot 25 - \int_0^{25} 6\sqrt{x}\, dx = 3000 - 500 = 2500$ dollars.

scoring_points.description: Sets up correct integral for profit

sample_responses.commentary: Revenue = price per meter × length; cost = integral of cost rate.
Equivalent form integrating profit rate per meter; also accepted.

required_elements: Sets up correct integral for profit

sample_responses.commentary: Evaluates cost rate at $x = 25$ instead of integrating; must integrate $6\sqrt{x}$ over $[0, 25]$.

parts.prompt: Using correct units, explain the meaning of $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\, dx$ in the context of this problem.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\, dx$ is the difference in cost, in dollars, of producing a cable of length 30 meters versus a cable of length 25 meters.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\,dx$ is the additional cost, in dollars, of producing the portion of cable between 25 and 30 meters from the beginning (i.e., the difference in production cost between a 30-meter cable and a 25-meter cable).

sample_responses.response: $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\,dx$ is the additional cost, in dollars, to produce the segment of cable from 25 to 30 meters from the beginning — equivalently, the difference in cost between a 30-meter and a 25-meter cable.
sample_responses.commentary: Interprets the integral of a cost rate as the accumulated cost over the interval $[25,30]$.

sample_responses.response: $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\,dx$ is the profit on the last 5 meters of cable.
sample_responses.commentary: This is only the cost portion, not profit; profit would also subtract revenue.

alternatives.correct_example: $\int_{25}^{30} 6\sqrt{x}\, dx$ is the difference in cost, in dollars, of producing a cable of length 30 meters and a cable of length 25 meters (i.e., the additional cost to extend the cable from 25 to 30 meters)

parts.prompt: Write an expression, involving an integral, that represents Mighty's profit on the sale of a cable that is $k$ meters long.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Profit $= 120k - \int_0^k 6\sqrt{x}\, dx$ dollars.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: includes the integral $\displaystyle\int_0^k 6\sqrt{x}\,dx$ for total production cost.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly sets up the total production cost as an integral.
Multiplies the cost rate at $x = k$ by the length; must integrate since cost rate varies with $x$.
Revenue minus cost expressed as an integral.
Subtracts in the wrong order; profit = revenue $-$ cost, not cost $-$ revenue.

parts.prompt: Find the maximum profit that Mighty could earn on the sale of one cable. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: $P'(k) = 120 - 6\sqrt{k} = 0 \Rightarrow k = 400$. Maximum profit $P(400) = 16000$ dollars. $P'$ changes from positive to negative at $k = 400$.

sample_responses.commentary: Differentiates $6\sqrt{k}$ as $6k$ instead of $6\sqrt{k}$; must use FTC correctly.
Forgot to square both sides; $\sqrt{k} = 20$ gives $k = 400$, not $k = \sqrt{20}$.
Uses the cost rate at $k = 400$ times $k$ instead of the integral; production cost $= 4 \cdot 400^{3/2} = 32000$, not $6\sqrt{400} \cdot 400 = 48000$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $P'(k) > 0$ for $0 < k < 400$ and $P'(k) < 0$ for $k > 400$, confirming a maximum; or equivalently, this is the only critical point and $P'' < 0$.

sample_responses.response: $P'(k) = 120 - 6\sqrt{k} > 0$ for $k < 400$ and $P'(k) < 0$ for $k > 400$, so $P$ has a maximum at $k = 400$.
sample_responses.commentary: Sign analysis of $P'$ confirms the only critical point is a maximum.

sample_responses.response: $k = 400$ is the only critical point, so it must be the maximum.
sample_responses.commentary: Insufficient justification; must show sign change of $P'$ or use second derivative to confirm it is a maximum.

alternatives.correct_example: $P'(k) > 0$ for $0 < k < 400$ and $P'(k) < 0$ for $k > 400$, so $P$ has a maximum at $k = 400$
